title: Algebra for Oscillators: Khovanskii Bases
speaker: Mateusz Michalek
abstract:
We will present recent applications of enumerative algebra to the study of stationary states in physics. Our point of departure are classical Newtonian differential equations with nonlinear potential. It turns out that the study of their stationary states is closely related to solving systems of special polynomial equations. As observed by physicists, the number of solutions is smaller than predicted by the Bezout bound or its celebrated refinement due to Bernstein and Kushnirenko.
We will present a generalization of these two bounds based on the theory of Khovanskii bases. Our results give the correct, expected numbers for the polynomial systems that inspired us to look at the more general theory.

proof_stages.label: Oscillator steady states as polynomial systems
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Duffing and coupled nonlinear oscillators, asks for the number of periodic steady states, and uses harmonic balance to turn truncated sine-cosine ansatz equations into polynomial systems in the unknown Fourier coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Classical BKK benchmark via toric images
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the Bernstein-Kushnirenko-Khovanskii theorem: for generic combinations of fixed monomials, solution counts are normalized volumes, proved by mapping by monomials, cutting the toric image by hyperplanes, and computing its degree.

proof_stages.label: Five-to-the-n anomaly from tied coefficients
proof_stages.summary: Applying naive monomial or Bezout expectations to the oscillator polynomial systems gives counts like 9^n, while numerical and one-particle computations give 5^n because the oscillator equations impose non-generic coefficient relations among monomials.

proof_stages.label: Non-monomial image-degree problem
proof_stages.summary: Re-running the BKK proof with polynomial generators still converts the equations into hyperplane sections of an image, but now the image is non-toric and computing its degree by Grobner elimination is generally too hard.

proof_stages.label: Khovanskii bases and leading-term replacement
proof_stages.summary: Khovanskii bases are introduced as algebra generators whose initial terms generate the whole initial algebra, with discussion of finite existence and an oscillator computation where the leading terms produce a polytope of volume five.

proof_stages.label: Grouped Khovanskii-basis mixed-volume theorem
proof_stages.summary: The general theorem is formulated for groups of polynomials mapping into products of projective spaces: under generically finite/injective map hypotheses and a Khovanskii-basis condition, isolated solution counts are bounded by, and generically equal to, a mixed volume of leading-term Newton polytopes.

proof_stages.label: Proof by flat toric degeneration
proof_stages.summary: The proof preserves the intersection-theoretic degree of the polynomial image under the flat degeneration supplied by the Khovanskii basis, reducing the count to the monomial toric case already handled by BKK volume computations.

proof_stages.label: Real counts, discriminants, and experiments
proof_stages.summary: The speaker then explains how real steady-state counts can change only through collisions on the discriminant, computes/compares discriminant curves with physics diagrams and experiments, and emphasizes that nonlinear potentials make these phase-change phenomena visible.

examples.name: One-particle Duffing harmonic-balance polynomial system
examples.note: The explicit two-polynomial system obtained from the truncated Duffing ansatz first demonstrates harmonic balance and then produces the key five-versus-nine discrepancy that forces the move from monomial BKK to Khovanskii bases.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker substitutes the harmonic-balance ansatz into the one-particle Duffing equation, integrates against sine and cosine, derives polynomial terms, and counts the resulting solutions

passes.reason: the one-particle uncoupled harmonic-balance system is a base case used to refute the naive nine-solution monomial/Bezout expectation

The one-particle harmonic-balance cubic system has constrained coefficients and common leading behavior at infinity giving five rather than the naive nine solutions, so it serves as a counterexample to naive generic-cubic or monomial-support counting.

The one-particle harmonic-balance system is a coefficient-tied cubic base case with common roots at infinity, attaining the sharp five-solution Khovanskii count while defeating the naive nine-solution Bézout/Newton expectation.
The one-particle harmonic-balance system is the minimal Duffing case and is used to show the naive Bézout/monomial-generic count overpredicts nine instead of five.
the one-particle harmonic-balance system is a special coefficient-coupled/base case with double points at infinity, used to show the naive Bezout or monomial-generic count overestimates
The one-particle harmonic-balance system is the n=1 uncoupled base case attaining the sharp five-solution count and showing the naive nine-solution monomial/Bezout expectation fails.
The one-particle harmonic-balance system is a sparse coefficient-tied minimal base case attaining the sharp five-solution bound while showing the naive nine-solution cubic/monomial count fails.
The one-particle harmonic-balance system has collapsed coefficient relations and is used to show that the naive monomial/Bézout count of nine overpredicts the actual count five.
The one-particle constrained cubic system is the base case whose coefficient relations make the naive Bezout/BKK count nine fail while generically attaining the sharp Khovanskii count five.
The one-particle cubic harmonic-balance system is the baby case attaining the five-solution Khovanskii bound while defeating the naive nine-solution support/Bezout expectation.

examples.name: Real discriminant phase diagrams for oscillators
examples.note: The parameter-plane pictures, including the later experimental blue plot, illustrate how collisions of real steady states across a discriminant explain observed changes in the number of real oscillator states.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes how real solutions collide on discriminant curves, computes/describes the discriminant and specializes it to draw phase diagrams compared with oscillator data

passes.reason: the discriminant phase diagrams describe critical boundary loci where steady-state solutions collide and become singular

The real discriminant phase diagrams are critical boundary loci where oscillator steady states collide and the number of real solutions changes, used to explain phase transitions rather than refute a claim.
The real discriminant diagrams are singular collision loci that form critical boundaries between regions with different numbers of real oscillator states.
The real discriminant phase diagrams are boundary loci where solutions collide and become singular, separating regions with different real solution counts.
